title: Qualitative Characteristics of Useful Information
event: An auditor flags a $45,000 misstatement against net income of $900,000, applying the common 5%-of-income materiality benchmark. Compute the misstatement as a percent of net income and determine whether it clears the benchmark.

computationPaths.narration: Materiality is relevance's threshold: information is material if omitting or misstating it would change a user's decision. Benchmarks like 5% of income operationalize the judgment.

memorize.body: Fundamental: Relevance (predictive value, confirmatory value, materiality) + Faithful Representation (complete, neutral, free from error). Enhancing: Comparability, Verifiability, Timeliness, Understandability — 'CVTU'.
Relevance asks 'does it matter?'; faithful representation asks 'is it true?'. Every framework question sorts into one of the two.
Materiality is entity-relative: $45,000 sinks a small firm's statements and rounds to zero at Apple. Percent-of-base, never absolute dollars.

questions.prompt: Misstatement $45,000, net income $900,000. Percent of net income = ?

distractors.feedback: Decimal slip — 45,000 ÷ 900,000 = 0.05 = 5%, right at the classic materiality line.
You inverted the ratio. The misstatement goes on TOP: 45 ÷ 900 = 5%.
